prompt: Translate the following English sentence into French, Japanese, and Arabic, and add a brief note on any phrase that was tricky to render faithfully: "The silence between us said more than any argument could have."
response:
French  
« Le silence entre nous en disait plus que n’aurait pu le faire aucune dispute. »  
Note: The verb phrase « en dire plus que » is the idiomatic way to keep the English sense of “say more than,” and the negative « n’aurait pu le faire » is needed to echo the past conditional “could have.”

Japanese  
私たちの間の沈黙は、どんな口論よりも多くを語っていた。  
Note: 語る (kataru) rather than 言う (iu) was chosen to convey “say” in the sense of “reveal/express,” and どんな口論よりも (donna kōron yori mo) is the natural comparative frame for “than any argument could have.”

Arabic  
الصمتُ بيننا قال أكثر ممّا كان يستطيع أيّ جدال أن يقوله.  
Note: The relative clause ممّا كان يستطيع … أن يقوله faithfully renders the English past potential “could have said,” but required splitting the verb “say” into يستطيع أن يقول to keep the conditional nuance.
